skill.name: performance-testing-review-ai-review
skill.description: Guides AI agents through pull request review workflows that combine static analysis, security checks, performance review, and AI-assisted comments.
skill.summary: Run structured AI-assisted code reviews with security, performance, architecture, and test coverage checks.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, filesystem, network, and secret-access patterns, but review confirms they are documentation examples and CI workflow snippets rather than hidden executable payloads. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious exfiltration was found. The skill still carries medium risk because it instructs agents to run external scanners, read CI secrets, send diff content to LLM APIs, and parse model output for automated review comments.

medium_findings.title: External Scanner and Subprocess Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill includes examples that run TruffleHog, sonar-scanner, CodeQL, Semgrep, jq, and Python subprocess calls. This is appropriate for a code review workflow, but an agent following the guidance may execute external tools against an untrusted repository or CI environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly present and semantically related to the skill purpose. Confidence is not higher because they are examples in Markdown, not packaged executable scripts.

medium_findings.title: Credential Use in Review Automation Examples
medium_findings.description: The examples read OPENAI_API_KEY, GITHUB_TOKEN, and ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from CI secrets or environment variables. No evidence shows exfiltration, but users must ensure secrets are only available in trusted CI contexts.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The secret access is explicit and tied to legitimate API clients. Confidence is moderate-high because the risk depends on how the workflow is deployed.

medium_findings.title: Untrusted Diff Content Sent to LLM Review Flow
medium_findings.description: The automation example places pull request diff content and static analysis results into an LLM prompt, then parses the model response as review issues. Malicious pull request content could try to manipulate the model output or review workflow unless callers add prompt-injection controls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The prompt construction and JSON parsing are visible in the example. Confidence is limited because there is no deployed script in the skill package.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Read Limited to Generated Review Comments
low_findings.description: The Node.js filesystem access reads review-comments.json inside a GitHub Actions example before posting review comments. This is a narrow local file read and does not show arbitrary path access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file read is explicit and scoped to one local report file. It is likely safe when the generated file is trusted.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Is a Documentation Reference
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL points to a CWE reference in an example review comment. It is not used as an exfiltration endpoint or runtime callback.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL appears in a references array for CWE-89 documentation. There is no surrounding network request using it.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Sensitive File Alerts Are Documentation Matches
low_findings.description: Static alerts for weak cryptography, Windows SAM, certificate files, and API keys are caused by prose such as OWASP category names, severity labels, CI secret placeholders, and example review fields. No evidence found of actual weak cryptographic implementation, Windows SAM access, or certificate file handling.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line-number review shows these are markdown front matter, vulnerability taxonomy text, or example fields. They do not form an executable sensitive-file or weak-crypto path.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command Execution Examples in CI and Python
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill demonstrates shell and subprocess execution for review tooling. These patterns are dangerous if copied into workflows that run on untrusted code without sandboxing or approval gates.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Command execution is present in concrete snippets. The surrounding context is legitimate CI automation, so this is a contextual safety warning rather than malicious code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Secret-Backed API Client Initialization
dangerous_patterns.description: The automation example initializes API clients from environment secrets. This is normal for CI but dangerous if secrets are exposed to forked pull requests or untrusted runners.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact secret variables are shown. There is no evidence of unauthorized transmission beyond intended API client use.

dangerous_patterns.title: LLM Output Parsed Into Review Actions
dangerous_patterns.description: The example parses model output into review issues after embedding diff content in the prompt. Automated consumers should validate and constrain output before posting comments or requesting changes.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The data flow from prompt construction to JSON parsing and review data assembly is visible. The package does not include an executable implementation, so exploitability depends on adoption.

content.user_title: Review Code with AI Performance and Security Checks
content.value_statement: Code review can miss security, performance, and architecture problems when teams rely on one tool. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured workflow for multi-tool AI-assisted review.
actual_capabilities: Triage pull requests by size, affected files, and security sensitivity.
Recommend static analysis tools such as CodeQL, SonarQube, Semgrep, Snyk, and TruffleHog.
Guide AI-assisted review prompts for security, performance, architecture, and testability checks.
Describe CI quality gates that block critical findings before merge.
Provide example review comment formats with severity, category, remediation, and references.
limitations: It is a guidance skill, not a packaged scanner or runnable review service.
Examples require adaptation before use in a production CI environment.
It does not guarantee complete vulnerability detection across every language.
External tool output and LLM responses still require human review.

use_cases.title: Review risky pull requests
use_cases.description: Use the workflow to prioritize security-sensitive changes and request focused AI review on risky files.

use_cases.title: Design CI review gates
use_cases.description: Map CodeQL, Semgrep, SonarQube, and dependency checks into a pull request quality gate.

use_cases.title: Improve review comments
use_cases.description: Standardize AI-generated review comments with severity, impact, remediation, and clear ownership.

prompt_templates.title: Review a small pull request
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this pull request for bugs, security issues, performance concerns, and missing tests. Keep findings concise and include file and line references when available.

prompt_templates.title: Create a static analysis plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Given this repository and pull request scope, choose the best static analysis tools and explain which risks each tool should cover.

prompt_templates.title: Assess performance regression risk
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this change for latency, memory, database, and scalability risks. Compare expected behavior against the current baseline and suggest targeted benchmarks.

prompt_templates.title: Build an AI review workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a CI-based AI code review process for this team. Include routing rules, security controls, quality gates, human approval points, and rollout metrics.

output_examples.input: A pull request changes login and session handling code.
output_examples.output: The review highlights authentication bypass risk, missing rate limits, weak session invalidation, and tests needed for failed login flows.

output_examples.input: A service adds database reads inside a loop.
output_examples.output: The review flags possible N+1 queries, recommends batched loading, and asks for a benchmark that measures latency at production-like volume.

output_examples.input: A team wants automated pull request quality gates.
output_examples.output: The workflow combines static analysis, dependency checks, secret scanning, AI review, and human approval for critical or security-sensitive changes.

best_practices: Run external scanners in isolated CI jobs with least-privilege tokens.
Treat pull request diffs as untrusted input when sending content to an LLM.
Require human approval for critical security, architecture, and data migration findings.
anti_patterns: Do not expose repository or API secrets to untrusted forked pull requests.
Do not let AI review output merge or block code without validation rules.
Do not rely on one scanner or one model for complete review coverage.

faq.question: Does this skill run scanners by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides review workflows, prompts, and CI examples that users must adapt and run in their own environment.

faq.question: Which tools does it mention?
faq.answer: It references CodeQL, SonarQube, Semgrep, Snyk, Dependabot, GitGuardian, TruffleHog, GitHub Actions, and LLM review tools.

faq.question: Can it review performance issues?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for latency, memory, database, scalability, benchmark, and regression review.

faq.question: Is it safe for private repositories?
faq.answer: Use caution. Review prompts may include proprietary code, so configure approved models, retention settings, and access controls.

faq.question: Does it replace human code review?
faq.answer: No. It is designed to assist reviewers and route high-risk changes to human approval.

faq.question: What setup is required?
faq.answer: Teams need their chosen scanners, CI permissions, model access, and policies for secrets and untrusted pull requests.
